tossups.question: The speaker states, “Destiny is your friend” in a work in this genre that notes, “We are the miracles that God made / To taste the bitter fruit of Time.” A work in this genre notes, “An anthem’s sentiment upholds the myth” after asking an author, “Are you grapevine wired?” This genre, which partly titles an “African” work by Ben Okri, was used for a work that addresses “POLITICIANS,” “THE EAGLES,” and “THE ROBBERS” in (*) all caps. The instruction “(with drum accompaniment)” opens a Christopher Okigbo poem in this genre during the Nigerian Civil War titled for Alto. A poem in this genre asks, “Who, if I cried out, would hear me among the Angelic Orders?” For 10 points, give this genre of Rainer Maria Rilke poems titled for Duino Castle.
tossups.question_sanitized: The speaker states, "Destiny is your friend" in a work in this genre that notes, "We are the miracles that God made / To taste the bitter fruit of Time." A work in this genre notes, "An anthem's sentiment upholds the myth" after asking an author, "Are you grapevine wired?" This genre, which partly titles an "African" work by Ben Okri, was used for a work that addresses "POLITICIANS," "THE EAGLES," and "THE ROBBERS" in (*) all caps. The instruction "(with drum accompaniment)" opens a Christopher Okigbo poem in this genre during the Nigerian Civil War titled for Alto. A poem in this genre asks, "Who, if I cried out, would hear me among the Angelic Orders?" For 10 points, give this genre of Rainer Maria Rilke poems titled for Duino Castle.
tossups.answer: elegy [or “Elegy For Alto”; or “Elegy for the Nation”; or “An African Elegy”; or Duino Elegies; prompt on poems] (the first two poems are by Ben Okri and Wole Soyinka respectively)
tossups.answer_sanitized: elegy [or "Elegy For Alto"; or "Elegy for the Nation"; or "An African Elegy"; or Duino Elegies; prompt on poems] (the first two poems are by Ben Okri and Wole Soyinka respectively)

tossups.question: The protagonist of a play titled for these figures justifies a murder by claiming that he found his victim “digging up my sweat!” on a farm. In a play titled for these figures, one character uses dark magic to force her daughter-in-law to drink poisoned water after discovering she is pregnant with another man; that play titled for the Wedlock of [these figures] was written by Zulu Sofola. While disguised as one of these figures, a driver stabs “Say-Tokyo-Kid” during a festival in the play The Road. The protagonist of a play titled for these figures uses a sleeping spell to kill three bodyguards and the “Old Man.” These figures title a play in which the protagonist, often referred to as “the Scorpion,” threatens a (*) seer by forcing him to feel a sword. In that play titled for these figures, that blind seer, Baba Fakunle, reveals that Odewale murdered King Adetusa. For 10 points, Ola Rotimi’s adaptation of Oedipus Rex is titled [what figures] Are Not to Blame?
tossups.question_sanitized: The protagonist of a play titled for these figures justifies a murder by claiming that he found his victim "digging up my sweat!" on a farm. In a play titled for these figures, one character uses dark magic to force her daughter-in-law to drink poisoned water after discovering she is pregnant with another man; that play titled for the Wedlock of [these figures] was written by Zulu Sofola. While disguised as one of these figures, a driver stabs "Say-Tokyo-Kid" during a festival in the play The Road. The protagonist of a play titled for these figures uses a sleeping spell to kill three bodyguards and the "Old Man." These figures title a play in which the protagonist, often referred to as "the Scorpion," threatens a (*) seer by forcing him to feel a sword. In that play titled for these figures, that blind seer, Baba Fakunle, reveals that Odewale murdered King Adetusa. For 10 points, Ola Rotimi's adaptation of Oedipus Rex is titled [what figures] Are Not to Blame?
tossups.answer: gods [accept The Gods Are Not to Blame; accept Wedlock of the Gods; prompt on orishas; anti-prompt on Ogun] (The Road is by Wole Soyinka.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: gods [accept The Gods Are Not to Blame; accept Wedlock of the Gods; prompt on orishas; anti-prompt on Ogun] (The Road is by Wole Soyinka.)

tossups.question: This author wrote a play about a family whose duty is to carry a dwarf boat out to the river once a year. This author created a group of women that chants “little children are our champions” after their daughters mock and chase away a policeman trying to stop a wedding. Jaguna’s trap kills the protagonist of a play by this author, in which a mentally challenged boy is replaced as the “carrier” by Eman. In a play by this author of The Strong Breed, the “mother of the market” allows the protagonist to marry and sleep with her son’s fiancée. Despite his assurances to the Praise-Singer in one of this author’s plays, the father of the medical student Olunde hesitates until Simon Pilkings stops him from committing ritual suicide. For 10 points, name this Nigerian author of Death and the King’s Horseman.
tossups.question_sanitized: This author wrote a play about a family whose duty is to carry a dwarf boat out to the river once a year. This author created a group of women that chants "little children are our champions" after their daughters mock and chase away a policeman trying to stop a wedding. Jaguna's trap kills the protagonist of a play by this author, in which a mentally challenged boy is replaced as the "carrier" by Eman. In a play by this author of The Strong Breed, the "mother of the market" allows the protagonist to marry and sleep with her son's fiancee. Despite his assurances to the Praise-Singer in one of this author's plays, the father of the medical student Olunde hesitates until Simon Pilkings stops him from committing ritual suicide. For 10 points, name this Nigerian author of Death and the King's Horseman.
tossups.answer_sanitized: Wole Soyinka [or Akinwande Oluwole Babatunde Soyinka]

packet.name: I - Georgia Tech A, Illinois A, Maryland A, Ohio State B, Penn A, Toronto B

tossups.question: In a novel by this author, three strangers approach Dr. Menka with an offer to buy human body parts; that 2021 novel is this author’s first published novel in 48 years. This author’s English translation helped popularize D. O. Fagunwa’s novel The Forest of a Thousand Daemons. This author discussed Nelson Mandela in his Nobel lecture “The Past Must Address Its Present.” In a play by this author, a character is disowned as an “eater of left-overs.” This author of (*) Chronicles from the Land of the Happiest People on Earth created a character who tells the story of the “Not-I Bird” to the Praise Singer. In a play by this author, a British district officer tries to prevent Elesin’s ritual suicide. For 10 points, name this Nigerian playwright of Death and the King’s Horseman.
tossups.answer_sanitized: Wole Soyinka [or Akinwande Oluwole Babatunde Soyinka]

tossups.question: In a play by this author, a group of four beggars who worship the god “As” help a former medic keep his father trapped in his clinic’s basement. Another play by this author ends after a young bride pours dirt over a dead man’s eyes. To spare a mute boy, the protagonist of a play by this author volunteers to take a boat to the ocean as a “carrier.” In a play by this author of (*) Madmen and Specialists, women in the market interrupt the protagonist as he tells the Praise-Singer the story of the “Not I” bird. This author’s play The Strong Breed depicts cultural rituals similar to his play in which Simon Pilkings tries to stop Elesin from committing ritual suicide. For 10 points, name this Nigerian playwright of Death and the King’s Horseman.
tossups.question_sanitized: In a play by this author, a group of four beggars who worship the god "As" help a former medic keep his father trapped in his clinic's basement. To spare a mute boy, the protagonist of a play by this author volunteers to take a boat to the ocean as a "carrier." In a play by this author of (*) Madmen and Specialists, women in the market interrupt the protagonist as he tells the Praise-Singer the story of the "Not I" bird. This author's play The Strong Breed depicts cultural rituals similar to his play in which Simon Pilkings tries to stop Elesin from committing ritual suicide. For 10 points, name this Nigerian playwright of Death and the King's Horseman.
tossups.answer_sanitized: Wole Soyinka ("WOL-ay shoy-een-KAH") [or Akinwande Oluwole Babatunde Soyinka]

tossups.question: In a novel from this non-Uruguayan country, four brothers behead a chicken as payback after a family friend reports their fishing at a cursed river to their mother. The “Gong of Four” opposes an organization that sells human body parts in a novel that satirizes this country as the “land of the happiest people on Earth.” A character from this country who arranges a green card marriage with Cleotilde is called “The Zed” by his friends and “Ceiling” by an old girlfriend. After her (*) Princeton fellowship ends, a character from this country writes about “The Hot White Ex” and “Professor Hunk” on her blog “Raceteenth” before returning to this country and reuniting with her high school flame, Obinze. For 10 points, name this home country of Chigozie Obioma and Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie.
tossups.answer: Nigeria [or Federal Republic of Nigeria] (The novels are Chigozie Obioma’s The Fishermen, Wole Soyinka’s Chronicles from the Land of the Happiest People on Earth, and Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie’s Americanah.)
tossups.answer_sanitized: Nigeria [or Federal Republic of Nigeria] (The novels are Chigozie Obioma's The Fishermen, Wole Soyinka's Chronicles from the Land of the Happiest People on Earth, and Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie's Americanah.)

tossups.question: An essay by this author holds that writers draw from a cultural matrix, or “From a Common Back Cloth.” An essay by this writer begins by advising “Pretenders to the crown of Pontifex Maximus of [a people’s] poetics [to] learn to mind the thorns” in rebutting a “troika” of critics. This author linked a “restorative” deity and Nietzsche’s (“NEE-chuh’s”) Apollonian–Dionysian dichotomy to the origin of tragedy in an essay that posits a ritual space called the (*) “Fourth Stage.” This author of “Neo-Tarzanism” countered Hegel and Leo Frobenius in the Nobel Lecture “The Past Must Address Its Present.” This author of a book about “Myth, Literature, and [his people’s] World” made the first English translation of the novel A Forest of a Thousand Demons. For 10 points, who added a chorus of slaves to his adaptation of Euripides’ Bacchae (“BAH-kye”), partly titled “A Communion Rite,” and wrote plays such as Kongi’s Harvest?
tossups.answer_sanitized: Wole Soyinka ("sho-YING-kuh") [or Akinwande Oluwole Babatunde Soyiinka]

tossups.question: Victor Ehikhamenor wrote “the British are back for more” after Damien Hirst copied a sculpture from this ethnicity for his Golden Heads (Female) , part of “Treasures from the Wreck of the Unbelievable.” This ethnicity’s distinction between an “inner head” and an “outer head” is exemplified by the three tiers of abstract faces that decorate their royal crowns. In his lecture “This Past Must Address Its Present,” a critic of this ethnicity criticized a racist German explorer who attributed the art of this ethnicity to a non-existent ancient (*) Greek colony. A sculptor from this ethnicity carved a pair of paneled doors that served as an entrance to a shrine for the king’s head. This ethnicity’s conical, beaded crowns include a veil that obscures the ruler’s face. One of the eighteen brass heads unearthed in 1938 may depict the legendary first ruler of this ethnicity, Odùduwà. For 10 points, name this ethnicity that created the heads discovered at Ile-Ife in what is now southwestern Nigeria.
tossups.question_sanitized: Victor Ehikhamenor wrote "the British are back for more" after Damien Hirst copied a sculpture from this ethnicity for his Golden Heads (Female) , part of "Treasures from the Wreck of the Unbelievable." This ethnicity's distinction between an "inner head" and an "outer head" is exemplified by the three tiers of abstract faces that decorate their royal crowns. In his lecture "This Past Must Address Its Present," a critic of this ethnicity criticized a racist German explorer who attributed the art of this ethnicity to a non-existent ancient (*) Greek colony. A sculptor from this ethnicity carved a pair of paneled doors that served as an entrance to a shrine for the king's head. This ethnicity's conical, beaded crowns include a veil that obscures the ruler's face. One of the eighteen brass heads unearthed in 1938 may depict the legendary first ruler of this ethnicity, Oduduwa. For 10 points, name this ethnicity that created the heads discovered at Ile-Ife in what is now southwestern Nigeria.

tossups.question: In a play by this author, a man apologizes to a Christian steward for calling holy water “nonsense.” One of this author’s characters asks, “Is it the parent shoot which withers to give sap to the younger?”, invoking the repeated image of a plantain. An aspiring doctor says “I have no father, eater of leftovers” at a masquerade attended by the Prince in a play by this author. One of his title characters chants the story of a bird who fled when “death came calling,” called the (*) “Not-I bird.” In one of this man’s plays, a chief feigns impotence to trick a beautiful girl into marrying him instead of the teacher Lakunle. In another of his plays, District Officer Simon Pilkings arrests Elesin Oba before he can commit ritual suicide. For 10 points, name this Nigerian playwright of The Lion and the Jewel and Death and the King’s Horseman.
tossups.question_sanitized: In a play by this author, a man apologizes to a Christian steward for calling holy water "nonsense." In another of his plays, District Officer Simon Pilkings arrests Elesin Oba before he can commit ritual suicide. For 10 points, name this Nigerian playwright of The Lion and the Jewel and Death and the King's Horseman.

tossups.question: While a woman and a fish seller squabble in a play by this author, the title character is able to escape through an open window and successfully evade one of the sinister “daughters of Eve.” After being told that people like to be remembered by what they leave behind, a character in a play by this author asks “who does not seek to be remembered? Memory is the master of death.” This author created a character who tells the tale of a bird that flies around the world to inform people of their impending (*) death, but each time receives the response “Not I.” Olunde mistakenly believes that a change in the sound of the drums means that his father has died in a play by this author. Simon Pilkings looks to put a stop to the ritual suicide of Elesin Oba in that play by this author. For 10 points, name this Nigerian playwright of Death and the King’s Horseman.
tossups.question_sanitized: While a woman and a fish seller squabble in a play by this author, the title character is able to escape through an open window and successfully evade one of the sinister "daughters of Eve." Olunde mistakenly believes that a change in the sound of the drums means that his father has died in a play by this author. Simon Pilkings looks to put a stop to the ritual suicide of Elesin Oba in that play by this author. For 10 points, name this Nigerian playwright of Death and the King's Horseman.

tossups.question: This author wrote that “I shall shoot you clean and fair / With meat and bread, a gourd of wine / A bunch of breasts from either arm, and that / Lone question - do you… know… / What it is all about?” in a poem depicting a wartime interaction between a “Civilian and Soldier.” The speaker of a poem by this author pleads “Madam… wouldn’t you rather / See for yourself?” after being asked several permutations of the all-caps question “ARE YOU DARK? OR VERY LIGHT?” by a potential landlady. This author wrote a volume of protest poems titled A Big (*) Airplane Crashed Into the Earth during a nearly two-year stint of imprisonment during which he scribbled a memoir on toilet paper and was unable to attend the Accra production of his play in which a “Dance of the Lost Traveller” is performed for a photographer who assembles a picture book from images of a village in which Lakunle and Baroka vie for the hand of Sidi. For 10 points, name this Nigerian author of “Telephone Conversation” and plays such as The Lion and the Jewel.
tossups.answer_sanitized: Wole Soyinka [or Akinwande Oluwole Babatunde Soyinka]
tossups.question_sanitized: This author wrote that "I shall shoot you clean and fair / With meat and bread, a gourd of wine / A bunch of breasts from either arm, and that / Lone question - do you... know... / What it is all about?" in a poem depicting a wartime interaction between a "Civilian and Soldier." The speaker of a poem by this author pleads "Madam... wouldn't you rather / See for yourself?" after being asked several permutations of the all-caps question "ARE YOU DARK? tossups.question: This author translated a fellow countryman’s early novel The Forest of a Thousand Daemons into English. A character created by this author enjoys the flavor of testicles and shoots his own cannibalistic father, who is known as The Old Man. Eman serves as the scapegoat “carrier” and is killed in this author’s play (*) The Strong Breed. In another play by this author, the chieftain of the town of Ilujinle fights over Sidi with the Westernized teacher Lakunle. For 10 points, name this Nigerian dramatist who wrote The Lion and the Jewel.
tossups.question_sanitized: This author translated a fellow countryman's early novel The Forest of a Thousand Daemons into English. In another play by this author, the chieftain of the town of Ilujinle fights over Sidi with the Westernized teacher Lakunle. For 10 points, name this Nigerian dramatist who wrote The Lion and the Jewel.

tossups.question: This author wrote the essay "Why Do I Fast?" about his time as a persona non grata; during that period he wrote "the man dies in all who keep silent in the face of tyranny" in a banned autobiography. One of his characters beats Shadrack in an election when his secretary Rebecca serves the voting prophets drinks in a play about his "Metamorphosis"; he introduced that charlatan preacher in an earlier play about his "Trials." This author wrote about a Westernized schoolteacher who refuses to pay the bride-price for Sidi in one play, and he focused another play on a suicide ritual carried out by Olunde in lieu of Elesin. For 10 points, name this Nigerian playwright of The Lion and the Jewel and Death and the King's Horseman.
tossups.question_sanitized: This author wrote the essay "Why Do I Fast?" about his time as a persona non grata; during that period he wrote "the man dies in all who keep silent in the face of tyranny" in a banned autobiography. This author wrote about a Westernized schoolteacher who refuses to pay the bride-price for Sidi in one play, and he focused another play on a suicide ritual carried out by Olunde in lieu of Elesin. For 10 points, name this Nigerian playwright of The Lion and the Jewel and Death and the King's Horseman.

tossups.question: In one work by this author, Brother Eleazar prophesies the death of his mentor, the title character, who is incorrectly proclaimed dead by a mob. The Old Man is imprisoned and then killed by his son, Dr. Bero, in another work by this author who wrote about a modernizing dictator who demands (*) Danlola’s family’s yams. The former play by this author was originally written as Die Still, Dr. Godspeak, but was revised as Requiem for a Futurologist. This author of Madmen and Specialists wrote about Sidi’s seduction by Baroka in one play, and he also wrote about Olunde’s suicide after Mr. Pilkings objects to the ritual sacrifice of Elesin. For 10 points, name this author of Kongi’s Harvest, The Lion and the Jewel, and Death and the King’s Horseman.

The former play by this author was originally written as Die Still, Dr. Godspeak, but was revised as Requiem for a Futurologist. For 10 points, name this author of Kongi's Harvest, The Lion and the Jewel, and Death and the King's Horseman.

tossups.question: This author wrote about a village scapegoat called Eman who is ritualistically killed to ward off evil, and in another work the god Aroni invites the Dead Man and Dead Woman to appear at The Gathering of the Tribes. This author of (*)The Strong Breed and A Dance of the Forests wrote short stories such as “The Banjo Broken String,” “Reptiles,” and “Madame Etienne’s Establishment” under the pen name Kongi. This author wrote a story in which the suicide of Elesin Oba is prevented by Simon Pilkings, and in another work by this author, the affections of Sidi are sought by the schoolteacher Lakunle and the chief Baroka. For 10 points, name this Nigerian playwright who penned Death and the King’s Horseman and The Lion and the Jewel.

This author wrote a story in which the suicide of Elesin Oba is prevented by Simon Pilkings, and in another work by this author, the affections of Sidi are sought by the schoolteacher Lakunle and the chief Baroka. For 10 points, name this Nigerian playwright who penned Death and the King's Horseman and The Lion and the Jewel.

bonuses.leadin: This poem commands, “Hear the fire’s voice, / Hear the voice of water” before describing “the sobbing of the trees” as the title phenomena of “our forefathers.” For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This poem commands, "Hear the fire's voice, / Hear the voice of water" before describing "the sobbing of the trees" as the title phenomena of "our forefathers." For 10 points each:
parts: Name this poem from Birago Diop’s collection Leurres et Lueurs, or Lures and Glimmers. Reflecting his Wolof upbringing, this poem uses the refrains, “listen more often to things rather than beings” and “the dead are not dead.”
Diop also wrote a collection of folktales titled for Amadou Koumba, who served in this role for his family. These oral historians and poets functioned as bards in West African culture.

“Breaths” was incorporated into a story from Tales of Amadou Koumba about a sergeant who becomes one of these people known as “Sarzan.” This is the first title group of a play in which Doctor Bero imprisons his father.
parts_sanitized: Name this poem from Birago Diop's collection Leurres et Lueurs, or Lures and Glimmers. Reflecting his Wolof upbringing, this poem uses the refrains, "listen more often to things rather than beings" and "the dead are not dead."
Diop also wrote a collection of folktales titled for Amadou Koumba, who served in this role for his family. These oral historians and poets functioned as bards in West African culture.

"Breaths" was incorporated into a story from Tales of Amadou Koumba about a sergeant who becomes one of these people known as "Sarzan." This is the first title group of a play in which Doctor Bero imprisons his father.
answers_sanitized: madman [or Madmen and Specialists (by Wole Soyinka)]

bonuses.leadin: This passage calls the “Colonial Factor” “an incident, a catalytic incident merely,” claiming instead that a coming “confrontation” is “metaphysical, contained in the human vehicle” and the universe of the mind. For 10 points each:

parts: Name this passage that attacks the “facile tag” of “clash of cultures” often applied to African literature.
This Nigerian author highlighted the “threnodic essence” of the action in the author’s note to his play Death and the King’s Horseman.
The author’s note relates how the story of this character, the King’s Horseman of the title, was inspired by a real-life incident in Oyo in 1946.
parts_sanitized: Name this passage that attacks the "facile tag" of "clash of cultures" often applied to African literature.
This Nigerian author highlighted the "threnodic essence" of the action in the author's note to his play Death and the King's Horseman.
The author's note relates how the story of this character, the King's Horseman of the title, was inspired by a real-life incident in Oyo in 1946.
answers: the author’s note to Death and the King’s Horseman [accept the preface to Death and the King’s Horseman]

Wole Soyinka [or Akinwande Oluwole Babatunde Soyinka]
Olori Elesin [or Elesin Oba; prompt on Olori]

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about Samuel Beckett’s use of spotlighting in his later experimental plays, for 10 points each.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Answer the following about Samuel Beckett's use of spotlighting in his later experimental plays, for 10 points each.
parts: In revisions to his play What Where, Beckett used a spotlight to illuminate the characters Bam, Bem, Bim, and Bom in lieu of their performance in this genre, which he had earlier used in his Act Without Words. Marcel Marceau created Bip the Clown for his stage performances in this genre.

A single, precisely arranged spotlight darts back and forth between three of these objects in Samuel Beckett’s play titled Play, in which the protagonists W1, W2, and M each sit in one of these specific objects.

A spotlight illuminates only the mouth of an actress throughout Beckett’s play of this title. In another play, the protagonist describes unrolling his welcome mat for a character of this name who augurs death.
parts_sanitized: In revisions to his play What Where, Beckett used a spotlight to illuminate the characters Bam, Bem, Bim, and Bom in lieu of their performance in this genre, which he had earlier used in his Act Without Words. Marcel Marceau created Bip the Clown for his stage performances in this genre.

bonuses.leadin: This man and Marianne Moore “collaborated” on a poem, for which Moore provided the title and this man wrote rhymes like “Terrell was big and ugly and tall / But when he fights me he is sure to fall.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name this man. Toni Morrison edited this boxer’s autobiography, The Greatest: My Own Story, a few months after Norman Mailer published The Fight about this man’s victory in the “Rumble in the Jungle.”
Mailer appeared in a documentary crediting Ali for writing the poem with the greatest amount of this quality in the world. A sad story about unworn baby shoes for sale notably has this quality.

brevity [or shortness or synonyms; accept being only six words] (That “world’s shortest poem” by Ali reads, “Me? Whee!!”)
Wole Soyinka [or Akinwande Oluwole Babatunde Soyinka] (The poem is “Telephone Conversation.”)

Soyinka created the prostitute Rola, who learns she was a queen in her past life, in a play titled for a “Dance of” these locations. Soyinka translated a D. O. Fagunwa novel titled for one of these locations “of a Thousand Daemons.”

parts: Name this animal whose arrival is signaled by a “rasp of reeds” and “whisper in the leaves” in a play in which the protagonist dances and chants the story of this animal in front of the women of the marketplace.
Elesin uses the Not-I bird’s story to reassure the women he will do his duty in this Nigerian author’s play Death and the King’s Horseman.

The Bird of the Wayside calls itself “a pair of women chatting” as it presents the prologue to a play by this author. The title woman drowns herself after discovering her husband’s infertility in this author’s play Anowa.
answers: Not-I bird [prompt on the bird from Death and the King’s Horseman; prompt on Death]
Ama Ata Aidoo [or Christina Ama Aidoo] (The first play is The Dilemma of a Ghost.)

bonuses.leadin: Many poems in Niyi Osundare’s collection The Eye of the Earth, such as “Harvest Call,” adapt the conventions of this genre to English poetry. For 10 points each:
parts: Give this genre whose forms include the Zulu izibongo, Tswana maboko, and Yoruba oriki. Named for an English verb, its poems often consist of a series of colorful epithets for a deity or ruler.
This Yoruba poet of “Telephone Conversation” wrote the oriki poem “Dawn,” one of his many works praising the orisha Ogun. A praise-singer appears in his play Death and the King’s Horseman.

Christopher Okigbo drew on the oriki form to eulogize this poet in “Lament of the Masks.” The couplet “In the prison of his days / Teach the free man how to praise” ends W. H. Auden’s poem eulogizing this poet.
answers: W. B. Yeats [or William Butler Yeats; accept “In Memory of W. B. Yeats”]
answers_sanitized: Wole Soyinka ("SHO-yin-kuh") [or Akinwande Oluwole Babatunde Soyinka]
W. B. Yeats [or William Butler Yeats; accept "In Memory of W. B. Yeats"]

bonuses.leadin: Characters in this play discuss how an official allowed the overloading of a truck that caught fire, leaving 65 people to burn to death. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this play, which was chosen as the official play of its country's independence celebrations. In a past life, two of its characters were killed by Emperor Mata Kharibu and his wife, Lady Tortoise.
A Dance of the Forests is by this Nigerian author, who explored the impacts of British colonialism on Nigeria in Death and the King's Horseman.

For Nigeria's independence, Soyinka also assembled an acting troupe named for the year 1960 and these objects. In Death and the King's Horseman, Amusa (ah-MOO-sah) is horrified when he sees District Officer Pilkings and his wife dancing while using some of these objects.

This Palestinian poet engaged with the racial politics of identity documents by repeating “Write down: I am an Arab” in the poem Identity Card.
This playwright wrote about asylum seekers in London in the radio play Document of Identity. This Nigerian playwright is better known for writing Death and the King’s Horseman.

bonuses.leadin: Many contemporary African playwrights have taken inspiration from Greek tragedy. For 10 points each:
parts: This Nigerian playwright of The Lion and the Jewel added a chorus of slaves in his adaptation of Euripides’s The Bacchae(“BAH-kay”), which is subtitled “a communion rite.”

This other Nigerian playwright adapted the title character of Oedipus Rex into Odewale (“OH-day-WAH-lay”), who banishes his brother Aderopo (“AH-day-ROH-poh”) from his kingdom after accusing him of bribing the seer Baba Fakunle (“fah-KOON-lay”).

A director complains about his inability to get white actors for a performance of this play in the prologue to a Femi Osofisan (“uh-SHOW-vee-zun”) adaptation. Two Robben Island prisoners put on a performance of this play in Athol Fugard, John Kani, and Winston Ntshona’s (“un-CHAW-nah’s”)The Island.
answers: Ola Rotimi (The play is The Gods Are Not to Blame.)
answers_sanitized: Wole Soyinka ("WOH-lay show-YIN-kuh") [or Akinwande Oluwole Babatunde Soyinka]
Ola Rotimi (The play is The Gods Are Not to Blame.)

Soyinka created this dictator of Isma, who demands the chief Oba Danlola’s submission. Segi presents the head of her father at the Festival of the New Yam to this character in a Soyinka play titled for his Harvest.

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about Obi Wali’s scathing report on the 1962 Conference of African Writers of English Expression, for 10 points each.
parts: Wali’s essay “The Dead End of African Literature” criticizes this author of The Lion and the Jewel for writing A Dance of the Forests in English, even though only 1 percent of his countrymen could understand it.

The essay “English and the African Writer” defended second-language writing in response to Wali. Examples from that essay included a speech from this novel, in which Chief Priest Ezeulu explains why he wants to send his son to experience the white man’s church.
Wali’s essay and Achebe’s response both discuss this other English-language Nigerian author. Achebe praises his poem “Night Rain,” while Wali quotes a negative review of his play Song of a Goat.

This Senegalese poet of "Midnight Elegy" was also featured in the collection. This co-founder of Negritude described a figure who is "clothed with your colour which is life" in "Black Woman."

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about playwrights and plays published in Heinemann’s African Writers Series, for 10 points each.
parts: In 1968, the AWS published this Ngũgĩ wa Thiong’o (“ung-goh-gay wa thee-ong-oh”) play that was the first play from East Africa to be published in English. This play’s protagonist is a young man named Remi, who becomes the first person in his village to attend a university.
This author’s novel The Interpreters was published in the AWS in 1970, although he is better known for his plays such as Death and the King’s Horseman and The Lion and the Jewel.

Cosmo Pieterse (“peter-zuh”), an author from this country, wrote and edited several anthologies of drama for the AWS. Although Bessie Head is commonly thought of as a Botswanan writer, she was actually born in this country.

bonuses.leadin: Description acceptable. Toloki finds a career doing this thing professionally in Zakes Mda’s (“muh-DAH’s”) Ways of Dying. For 10 points each:

Elesin’s son Olunde commits ritual suicide to mourn the death of their king in Death and the King’s Horseman, a play by this author.
answers: mourning the dead [accept mourning a family member or attending funerals; be lenient]

bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Description acceptable. Toloki finds a career doing this thing professionally in Zakes Mda's ("muh-DAH's") Ways of Dying. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this process, which requires the father of Olunde to commit ritual suicide in another work.
Tambu fails to mourn the death of her older brother Nhamo because it means there will be money for her to go to missionary school in this novel by Tsitsi Dangarembga ("tuh-SIT-see dan-gar-EMB-gah").
Elesin's son Olunde commits ritual suicide to mourn the death of their king in Death and the King's Horseman, a play by this author.

bonuses.leadin: The first novel to be published in the language of this people is framed a hunter telling stories of his exploits in the title Forest of a Thousand Daemons. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this ethnic group. A writer from this ethnic group described a man who travels to Dead’s Town to retrieve his deceased tapster.
Amos Tutuola drew on Yoruba folklore for his novel titled for a “drinkard” of this substance. In Things Fall Apart, Unoka is considered lazy in part because he consumes too much of this substance.
Forest of a Thousand Daemons was translated into English by this Yoruba playwright of Death and the King's Horseman.
answers: Yoruba people [Forest of a Thousand Daemons is by D.O. Fagunwa.]
answers_sanitized: Yoruba people [Forest of a Thousand Daemons is by D.O. Fagunwa.]
palm wine [prompt on wine or alcohol]

bonuses.leadin: This character has his armpit hairs plucked one by one. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this chieftain, the father of 63 children at the age of 62, whose main wife is Sadiku and who pursues the young Sidi.
Baroka is the title “animal” of this play. He competes with the Western-educated teacher Lakunle to marry the aforementioned beautiful woman, who he ultimately adds to his harem.
The Lion and the Jewel was written by this Nigerian Nobel laureate, the author of Death and the King’s Horseman.
answers: Baroka [prompt on, but DO NOT REVEAL, the lion]
answers_sanitized: Baroka [prompt on, but DO NOT REVEAL, the lion]
Wole Soyinka (WALL-ay SHOW-ying-ka) [or Akinwande Oluwole Babatunde Soyinka]

bonuses.leadin: This author satirized Idi Amin as the tyrannical Dr. Kamini in 1984’s A Play of Giants. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Nobel-winning Nigerian playwright who also criticized dictators in works like King Baabu and Kongi’s Harvest.
A personality disorder leads Christopher to believe he is Idi Amin’s son in a Joe Penhall play titled for these two colors that Christopher mistakes for one another.

Chiwetel Ejiofor (“CHEW-uh-tell EDGE-ee-oh-for”), who played Christopher in the premiere of Blue/Orange, starred as Patrice Lumumba in The Young Vic’s 2013 production of this 1966 play that concerns Lumumba’s fall and eventual assassination.
answers: A Season in the Congo [or Une saison au Congo] (by Aimé Césaire)
answers_sanitized: Wole Soyinka [or Akinwande Oluwole Babatunde Soyinka]
blue and orange [accept in either order; accept Blue/Orange]
A Season in the Congo [or Une saison au Congo] (by Aime Cesaire)

Chiwetel Ejiofor ("CHEW-uh-tell EDGE-ee-oh-for"), who played Christopher in the premiere of Blue/Orange, starred as Patrice Lumumba in The Young Vic's 2013 production of this 1966 play that concerns Lumumba's fall and eventual assassination.

bonuses.leadin: This character insists on keeping his “followers dissatisfied, because if they are satisfied, they won’t come again.” For 10 points each:
parts: Name character who gets some prophets drunk to defeat Shadrack in an election in a play about his “Metamorphosis.” He convinces his assistant Chume to beat his wife in another play.
This Nigerian author wrote The Trials of Brother Jero and Death and the King’s Horseman.

This protagonist of Soyinka’s Death and the King’s Horseman uses the story of the “Not-I” bird to convince the Praise-Singer of his readiness to commit suicide, which he ends up doing by hanging himself with his own chains.
answers: Elesin Oba [accept either name; prompt on the King’s Horseman]
